docs.title: Nexar
docs.text:
Intelligent Quantum-Classical Code Routing Platform

Nexar is an intelligent platform that automatically analyzes classical code and determines whether it would benefit from quantum execution. It inspects the structure, complexity, and algorithmic patterns of submitted code, predicts the optimal execution target -- classical or quantum -- and translates the code into quantum circuits when advantageous. Nexar then routes the compiled job to the most suitable quantum hardware provider, abstracting away the complexity of multi-vendor quantum backends.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The platform processes each request through a six-step pipeline:

  1. Submit -- The user submits code through the React frontend.
  2. Route -- The API Gateway authenticates the request and proxies it to the appropriate backend service.
  3. Analyze -- The Code Analysis Engine inspects the code for language, complexity metrics, algorithmic patterns, and quantum properties.
  4. Decide -- The Decision Engine predicts the optimal execution target (classical or quantum) based on the analysis results.
  5. Convert -- If quantum execution is selected, the AI Code Converter translates the classical Python code into quantum circuits.
  6. Execute -- The Hardware Abstraction Layer submits the job to the selected quantum provider (IBM Qiskit, AWS Braket, or Azure Quantum).

docs.title: Rule-Based System (6-Step Evaluation)
docs.text:
The rule system can force a decision (bypassing ML and cost) or defer to the weighted merger.

Input
  │
  ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ STEP 1: Hardware Compatibility                                   │
│                                                                  │
│  Quantum checks:          Classical checks:                      │
│  • qubits ≤ 127           • memory ≤ 512 GB                     │
│  • circuit_depth ≤ 10000  • problem_size ≤ 1,000,000            │
│  • gate_count ≤ 100000                                          │
│  • qubits ≥ 2                                                   │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌─── Neither compatible? ──▶ REJECT                            │
│  └─── Only one compatible? ──▶ FORCE that hardware (Step 3)     │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ both compatible
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ STEP 2: Safety Constraints                                       │
│                                                                  │
│  • Circuit volume (qubits × depth) ≤ 1,000,000                  │
│  • Noise sensitivity (qubits × depth × cx_ratio) ≤ 50,000       │
│  • NISQ viability P(success) ≥ 0.01                              │
│      P(success) = P(gates) × P(readout) × P(decoherence)        │
│      ├─ P(gates)       = (1-e_2q)^n_2q × (1-e_1q)^n_1q         │
│      ├─ P(readout)     = (1-e_ro)^n_qubits                      │
│      └─ P(decoherence) = exp(-circuit_time / T2)                 │
│                                                                  │
│  Uses live IBM device calibration from HAL:                      │
│    median_cx_error, median_sx_error, median_readout_error,       │
│    median_t2_us, median_gate_time_ns                             │
│                                                                  │
│  Exception: if qubits > 40, classical sim is infeasible          │
│  (2^40+ amplitudes) so quantum is allowed despite low viability  │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌─── Violated? ──▶ FORCE_CLASSICAL                             │
└──┴───────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ safe
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ STEP 3: Clear-Cut Rules                                          │
│                                                                  │
│  • Only quantum compatible?  ──▶ FORCE_QUANTUM                   │
│  • Only classical compatible? ──▶ FORCE_CLASSICAL                │
│  • problem_size < 10 AND qubits < 5? ──▶ FORCE_CLASSICAL        │
│    (too small for quantum overhead)                              │
│  • qubits == 0 OR (superposition < 0.1 AND entanglement < 0.1)  │
│    ──▶ FORCE_CLASSICAL (no quantum features)                     │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌─── Rule matched? ──▶ Return forced decision                  │
└──┴───────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ no clear-cut rule
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ STEP 4: Quantum Advantage Threshold                              │
│                                                                  │
│  Feature Score (weighted):                                       │
│    score = superposition×0.4 + entanglement×0.4 + cx_ratio×0.2  │
│                                                                  │
│  Strong features? (need ONE of):                                 │
│    • score ≥ 0.65                                                │
│    • 3+ indicators met:                                          │
│        - superposition ≥ 0.6                                     │
│        - entanglement ≥ 0.5                                      │
│        - cx_gate_ratio ≥ 0.2                                     │
│        - EXPONENTIAL complexity                                  │
│        - QUADRATIC_SPEEDUP complexity                            │
│                                                                  │
│  Practical scale? (need ONE of):                                 │
│    • qubits ≥ 20  (classical sim becomes expensive)              │
│    • problem_size ≥ 500 AND complexity is                        │
│      EXPONENTIAL or QUADRATIC_SPEEDUP                            │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌─── Strong features AND practical scale?                       │
│  │    ──▶ FORCE_QUANTUM                                          │
│  └─── Otherwise: continue (features noted but no override)       │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ no quantum advantage
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ STEP 5: Problem-Type Routing                                     │
│                                                                  │
│  Problem Type       Preferred    Reason                          │
│  ─────────────────  ──────────   ─────────────────────────────── │
│  FACTORIZATION      Quantum      Shor's algorithm (exp speedup)  │
│  SEARCH             Quantum      Grover's (quadratic speedup)    │
│  SIMULATION         Quantum      Natural quantum simulation      │
│  OPTIMIZATION       Quantum      QAOA/VQE exploration            │
│  SORTING            Classical    Classical sorting is optimal     │
│  DYNAMIC_PROG       Classical    Sequential dependencies         │
│  MATRIX_OPS         Classical    Mature classical libraries       │
│                                                                  │
│  Quantum-preferred types also require:                           │
│    • Minimum qubits: FACTOR ≥ 8, SEARCH ≥ 4, SIM ≥ 6, OPT ≥ 6 │
│    • Practical scale: qubits ≥ 20 to FORCE quantum              │
│      (< 20 qubits → ALLOW_BOTH, defer to weighted merger)       │
│                                                                  │
│  Classical-preferred types always FORCE_CLASSICAL                 │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌─── Quantum + large scale? ──▶ FORCE_QUANTUM                  │
│  ├─── Quantum + small scale? ──▶ ALLOW_BOTH (defer to merger)   │
│  ├─── Classical preferred?   ──▶ FORCE_CLASSICAL                 │
│  └─── No match?             ──▶ continue to Step 6              │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ no problem-specific rule
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ STEP 6: Default — ALLOW_BOTH                                     │
│                                                                  │
│  No rule applies. Return decision_type = ALLOW_BOTH              │
│  ──▶ Defer to weighted merger (ML 50% + Rules 35% + Cost 15%)   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.text:
Rule Decision
                        │
                        ▼
              ┌─────────────────────┐
              │  FORCE_QUANTUM or   │──── yes ──▶ Return rule override
              │  FORCE_CLASSICAL or │             (bypasses all scoring)
              │  REJECT?            │
              └─────────┬───────────┘
                        │ no (ALLOW_BOTH)
                        ▼
              ┌─────────────────────┐
              │  Weighted Scoring   │
              │                     │

              │  ┌────────────────┐ │
              │  │ ML score:      │ │
              │  │  conf × 0.50   │ │
              │  ├────────────────┤ │
              │  │ Rule score:    │ │
              │  │  conf × 0.35   │ │
              │  │  (0.175 each   │ │
              │  │  if ALLOW_BOTH)│ │
              │  ├────────────────┤ │

              │  │  if agrees     │ │

              │  └────────────────┘ │
              │                     │
              │  total = sum(above) │
              └─────────┬───────────┘
                        │
                        ▼
              ┌─────────────────────┐
              │  Highest total wins │

docs.title: Cost Analyzer Formulas
docs.text:
QUANTUM TIME:
  base_circuit_time  = circuit_depth × 0.1ms
  gate_time          = gate_count × 0.05ms
  shot_overhead      = 1024 shots × 0.01ms
  × problem_type_overhead (0.9–1.5)
  × complexity_multiplier (0.5–2.0)
  × NISQ_overhead (1.2)
  + queue_time (50–500ms by qubit count)

QUANTUM COST:
  $0.30 base/task
  + 1024 × $0.00145/shot
  + (time_ms/1000) × $1.60/sec
  + gate_count × $0.0001/gate

CLASSICAL TIME:
  EXPONENTIAL:  problem_size² × 0.1ms
  POLYNOMIAL:   problem_size^1.5 × 0.05ms
  NLOGN:        problem_size × log₂(size) × 0.01ms
  DEFAULT:      problem_size × 0.1ms
  + memory_mb × 0.01ms
  × problem_type_multiplier (0.5–5.0)

CLASSICAL COST:
  (time_ms/1000) × $0.0001/sec
  + (memory_gb) × (time_ms/1000) × $0.00001/GB·s
  + $0.001 overhead

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
Nexar is an intelligent quantum-classical code routing platform. It accepts source code from users, analyses it across multiple dimensions (language, complexity, algorithm type, quantum suitability), decides whether the code should run on quantum or classical hardware, optionally converts it between paradigms, and executes it on the most appropriate backend — all through a unified web interface.

The system is built as a microservices monorepo with six independently deployable services plus a React frontend, deployed to Google Cloud Platform via Terraform.

docs.title: Typical Request Flow
docs.text:
1. A user submits code through the Frontend.
  2. The API Gateway authenticates the request and proxies it to the appropriate backend service.
  3. The Code Analysis Engine inspects the code — detecting the language, computing complexity metrics, identifying algorithms, and extracting quantum-specific properties.
  4. The Decision Engine consumes the analysis output and predicts the optimal execution target (quantum device, quantum simulator, or classical hardware).
  5. If quantum execution is chosen, the AI Code Converter can translate classical Python code into quantum circuits (Qiskit / Cirq).
  6. The Hardware Abstraction Layer submits the job to the selected provider and returns results.

docs.title: 3.1 Frontend
docs.text:
Location /frontend Stack React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui Port 5173

The frontend is a single-page application that provides the user-facing interface for the entire platform. Key capabilities include:

  • Code submission and analysis — syntax-highlighted editor with real-time results display.
  • Decision visualisation — presents the routing recommendation (quantum vs. classical) with supporting metrics.
  • Hardware monitoring — live view of available quantum devices, their status, and specifications.
  • Execution history — tracks past jobs with results and performance data.
  • AI code conversion — interface for translating Python to quantum code and analysing quantum suitability.
  • AST pattern analyser — visual tool for inspecting quantum-amenable patterns in code.

docs.title: 3.2 API Gateway
docs.text:
Location /api Stack Express 5, TypeScript, Axios, Winston Port 3000

The gateway is the single entry point for all client requests. It provides:

  • Authentication — user registration, login, email verification, and Google OAuth, backed by JWT tokens and Firebase/Firestore.
  • Request proxying — forwards requests to the four Python backend services based on URL path.
  • Logging and monitoring — assigns a unique request ID to every call, logs upstream response times, and flags slow responses.
  • Protected routes — all backend service endpoints require a valid JWT; only auth routes are public.

docs.title: 3.3 Code Analysis Engine
docs.text:
Location /code-analysis-engine Stack FastAPI, Python, CodeBERT, scikit-learn, tree-sitter Port 8002

This service is the analytical core of the platform. Given a piece of source code, it produces a rich set of metrics used downstream by the Decision Engine:

  • Language detection — identifies Python, Qiskit, Cirq, Q#, and OpenQASM with confidence scores.
  • Classical metrics — cyclomatic complexity, nesting depth, loop/conditional counts, and time/space complexity estimation via AST analysis.
  • Quantum metrics — qubit requirements, circuit depth (via dependency graph), gate counts, superposition and entanglement scoring (via state simulation), and CX gate ratio.
  • Algorithm classification — a three-tier approach: (1) CodeBERT transformer for semantic multi-label classification, (2) ML ensemble classifier fallback, (3) rule-based pattern matching.
  • Optimisation suggestions — actionable recommendations with expected improvement estimates covering maintainability, performance, and resource efficiency.

docs.title: 3.4 Decision Engine
docs.text:
Location /decision-engine Stack FastAPI, Python, scikit-learn Port 8003

The Decision Engine receives the metrics produced by the Code Analysis Engine and predicts the optimal execution platform. It uses trained ML classification models that consider problem type, complexity, qubit count, gate profile, memory requirements, and time complexity to recommend one of: a real quantum device, a quantum simulator, or classical hardware. The service also exposes model metadata so operators can inspect the active model version and its configuration.

docs.title: 3.5 AI Code Converter
docs.text:
Location /ai-code-converter Stack FastAPI, Python, Transformers, Qiskit, Cirq Port 8001

This service bridges the gap between classical and quantum code:

  • Code translation — converts classical Python into quantum circuits targeting Qiskit or Cirq, using transformer-based code generation.
  • Quantum pattern recognition — detects patterns amenable to quantum speedup (e.g., linear search → Grover's, optimisation → QAOA, factorisation → Shor's).
  • Suitability scoring — assigns a quantum suitability level (HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW) with confidence scores and expected speedup estimates.
  • Circuit execution — runs generated quantum circuits and returns measurement distributions, circuit diagrams, and performance metrics.
  • Logic extraction — strips boilerplate from submitted code to isolate the core logic function for cleaner analysis.

docs.title: 3.6 Hardware Abstraction Layer
docs.text:
Location /hardware-abstraction-layer Stack FastAPI, Python, Qiskit, Amazon Braket SDK, Azure Quantum SDK Port 8004

The HAL provides a provider-agnostic interface to quantum computing backends:

  • Multi-provider support — IBM Qiskit (real devices and simulators), AWS Braket, and Azure Quantum.
  • Device management — lists available devices per provider with specifications (qubit count, status, connectivity, gate set).
  • Job execution — submits quantum circuits or classical code to the selected backend, supporting configurable shot counts.
  • Job lifecycle — tracks job status, retrieves results, and monitors execution performance.
  • Scheduling and availability — schedules quantum jobs and checks device availability windows.

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text: Nexar is an intelligent quantum-classical code routing platform that analyses user-submitted code, determines whether it should run on a quantum device, a quantum simulator, or a classical machine, and executes it on the most appropriate backend. The platform is built as a microservices monorepo deployed on Google Cloud Platform.

docs.title: Microservices Layout
docs.text:
The Nexar monorepo contains six independently deployable services. Each service owns its own Dockerfile, dependency manifest, and configuration, allowing teams to develop, test, and release each component on its own cadence. Inter-service communication happens over HTTP, with the API Gateway acting as the sole ingress point for external traffic and routing requests to the appropriate backend service.

Service Directory Port Stack Purpose Frontend /frontend 5173 React 18, TypeScript, Vite User interface for code submission, analysis, and results API Gateway /api 3000 Express 5, TypeScript Authentication, request proxying, logging Code Analysis Engine /code-analysis-engine 8002 FastAPI, Python Language detection, complexity analysis, algorithm classification Decision Engine /decision-engine 8003 FastAPI, Python ML-based quantum vs. classical routing AI Code Converter /ai-code-converter 8001 FastAPI, Python Classical-to-quantum code translation Hardware Abstraction Layer /hardware-abstraction-layer 8004 FastAPI, Python Provider-agnostic quantum backend interface

docs.title: Request Flow
docs.text:
A typical request passes through the platform in six steps:

  1. Code Submission -- The user writes or pastes code into the Frontend editor and submits it for analysis. The Frontend sends the code to the API Gateway over HTTPS.

  2. Authentication and Routing -- The API Gateway validates the user's JWT, attaches a unique request ID for tracing, and forwards the payload to the Code Analysis Engine.

  3. Code Analysis -- The Code Analysis Engine detects the programming language, computes classical metrics (cyclomatic complexity, nesting depth, time/space complexity), extracts quantum metrics (qubit requirements, circuit depth, gate counts), and classifies the algorithm using a three-tier approach (CodeBERT transformer, ML ensemble fallback, rule-based pattern matching). The resulting analysis report is returned to the API Gateway.

  4. Routing Decision -- The API Gateway forwards the analysis report to the Decision Engine, which builds a feature vector from the metrics and runs it through a trained classification model. The model outputs the optimal execution target: a real quantum device, a quantum simulator, or a classical machine.

  5. Code Conversion and Execution -- If the decision targets a quantum backend and the submitted code is classical, the API Gateway sends it to the AI Code Converter, which translates it into a quantum circuit (Qiskit or Cirq). The resulting circuit (or the original code, if already quantum) is then submitted to the Hardware Abstraction Layer, which dispatches the job to the selected provider (IBM Qiskit, AWS Braket, or Azure Quantum).

  6. Results Delivery -- Execution results, including measurement distributions and performance metrics, flow back through the API Gateway to the Frontend, where they are rendered alongside the analysis report and routing rationale.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
Nexar is deployed on Google Cloud Platform using Cloud Run for each service. Infrastructure is managed as code with Terraform, with all configuration stored in the /terraform directory at the repository root.

  • Containerisation -- Every service has its own Dockerfile. Images are built in CI and pushed to Google Artifact Registry.
  • CI/CD -- GitHub Actions workflows lint, test, build, and deploy each service independently. Pull requests trigger preview deployments; merges to main trigger production deployments.
  • Infrastructure as Code -- Terraform modules define Cloud Run services, networking, IAM policies, and secret management, ensuring reproducible environments across staging and production.

docs.title: AI Code Converter
docs.text: Directory /ai-code-converter Stack FastAPI, Python, Transformers, Qiskit, Cirq Port 8001

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The AI Code Converter bridges classical and quantum code within the Nexar platform. It translates classical Python into quantum circuits, evaluates code for quantum suitability, executes generated circuits, and returns results. This service is invoked when the Decision Engine determines that code should run on a quantum backend but the submitted code is classical.

docs.title: Code Translation
docs.text: The converter translates classical Python code into quantum circuits targeting Qiskit or Cirq. Translation is powered by a transformer-based generation model that has been trained on paired classical-quantum code examples. The model analyses the logic structure of the input code and produces an equivalent quantum circuit that preserves the computational intent while leveraging quantum parallelism where applicable.

docs.title: Pattern Recognition
docs.text:
Before translation, the converter scans the input code for well-known quantum-amenable patterns. Recognised patterns are mapped to established quantum algorithms:

  • Linear search maps to Grover's algorithm, offering quadratic speedup for unstructured search problems.
  • Combinatorial optimisation maps to QAOA (Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm), targeting problems like MaxCut and constraint satisfaction.
  • Integer factorisation maps to Shor's algorithm, providing exponential speedup over classical factorisation methods.

When a pattern is detected, the converter uses the corresponding quantum algorithm template rather than a generic translation, producing more efficient circuits.

docs.title: Suitability Scoring
docs.text:
For every input, the converter produces a suitability assessment indicating how well the code maps to quantum execution:

  • HIGH -- The code contains patterns with known quantum advantage; significant speedup is expected.
  • MEDIUM -- The code can be expressed as a quantum circuit, but the advantage over classical execution is modest or depends on problem scale.
  • LOW -- The code does not benefit meaningfully from quantum execution.

Each rating is accompanied by a confidence score and an expected speedup estimate (e.g., quadratic, exponential, or negligible), giving users clear expectations before committing to execution.

docs.title: Circuit Execution
docs.text:
The converter can execute generated quantum circuits directly, returning:

  • Measurement distributions -- The probability distribution over measurement outcomes across the configured number of shots.
  • Performance metrics -- Execution time, shot count, circuit depth after transpilation, and gate counts on the target backend.

This capability allows users to test converted circuits without leaving the conversion interface.

docs.title: Logic Extraction
docs.text: Before translation, the converter applies a logic extraction step that strips boilerplate code (imports, I/O handling, argument parsing, logging) to isolate the core computational logic. This ensures that the translation model focuses on the algorithmically meaningful portion of the code, producing cleaner and more efficient quantum circuits.

docs.title: API Gateway
docs.text: Directory /api Stack Express 5, TypeScript, Axios, Winston Port 3000

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The API Gateway is the single entry point for all client requests into the Nexar platform. It sits between the React frontend and the four Python backend services, handling authentication, request proxying, structured logging, and monitoring. No backend service is exposed directly to the internet; all external traffic passes through this gateway.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
The gateway implements a full authentication layer built on JWT tokens with Firebase and Firestore as the backing identity store.

  • User Registration and Login -- Users can create accounts with email and password. Passwords are hashed and stored securely. On successful login, the gateway issues a signed JWT that the client includes in subsequent requests.
  • Email Verification -- New accounts receive a verification email. Unverified accounts have restricted access until the email address is confirmed.
  • Google OAuth -- Users can authenticate via Google OAuth as an alternative to email/password registration, streamlining onboarding.
  • Token Management -- JWTs carry user identity and role claims. The gateway validates tokens on every protected request, rejecting expired or malformed tokens with appropriate HTTP status codes.

docs.title: Request Proxying
docs.text:
After authentication, the gateway forwards requests to one of the four Python backend services based on the URL path. Axios is used as the HTTP client for upstream calls. Each proxy route maps a public API path to the corresponding internal service endpoint:

  • Code Analysis Engine (port 8002)
  • Decision Engine (port 8003)
  • AI Code Converter (port 8001)
  • Hardware Abstraction Layer (port 8004)

The gateway preserves the original request body and headers, appending internal tracing headers before forwarding.

docs.title: Logging and Monitoring
docs.text:
Every inbound request is assigned a unique request ID that propagates through all upstream calls, enabling end-to-end tracing across services. Winston provides structured, levelled logging with the following capabilities:

  • Request/Response Logging -- Method, path, status code, and timing are logged for every request.
  • Upstream Response Time -- The gateway records how long each backend service takes to respond, surfacing latency in logs.
  • Slow Response Flagging -- Responses that exceed a configurable threshold are flagged in the logs, making it straightforward to identify performance regressions.

docs.title: Protected Routes
docs.text: All routes that proxy to backend services require a valid JWT. The only public (unauthenticated) endpoints are the authentication routes themselves: registration, login, OAuth callbacks, and email verification. This ensures that no analysis, conversion, or execution request can reach the backend without an authenticated user context.

docs.title: Code Analysis Engine
docs.text: Directory /code-analysis-engine Stack FastAPI, Python, CodeBERT, scikit-learn, tree-sitter Port 8002

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Code Analysis Engine is the analytical core of the Nexar platform. It accepts raw source code, determines the programming language, computes a comprehensive set of classical and quantum metrics, classifies the algorithm, and returns a structured analysis report. This report serves as the primary input to the Decision Engine, which uses it to determine the optimal execution target.

docs.title: Language Detection
docs.text:
The engine identifies the programming language of submitted code, supporting:

  • Python -- General-purpose Python code
  • Qiskit -- IBM's quantum computing SDK (Python-based)
  • Cirq -- Google's quantum computing framework (Python-based)
  • Q# -- Microsoft's quantum programming language
  • OpenQASM -- Open Quantum Assembly Language

Each detection result includes a confidence score indicating the engine's certainty. When quantum-specific imports or syntax are present, the detector distinguishes between plain Python and quantum framework code.

docs.title: Classical Metrics
docs.text:
For all submitted code, the engine computes classical complexity metrics through AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) analysis:

  • Cyclomatic Complexity -- Measures the number of independent paths through the code, indicating decision complexity.
  • Nesting Depth -- Tracks the maximum depth of nested control structures (loops, conditionals, try/except blocks).
  • Loop and Conditional Counts -- Enumerates the number of for/while loops and if/else branches.
  • Time Complexity -- Estimates asymptotic time complexity (e.g., O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2)) by analysing loop structures and recursive patterns.
  • Space Complexity -- Estimates memory usage growth based on data structure allocations and recursive call depth.

docs.title: Quantum Metrics
docs.text:
When the code involves quantum operations, the engine extracts additional metrics:

  • Qubit Requirements -- The number of qubits needed to execute the circuit.
  • Circuit Depth -- Computed via a dependency graph of gate operations, representing the critical path length.
  • Gate Counts -- Total number of quantum gates, broken down by type (Hadamard, CNOT, Toffoli, rotation gates, etc.).
  • Superposition and Entanglement Scoring -- Quantifies the degree of superposition (Hadamard density) and entanglement (multi-qubit gate density) in the circuit.
  • CX Gate Ratio -- The proportion of CNOT (CX) gates relative to total gates, an important factor in estimating noise impact on real hardware.

docs.title: Algorithm Classification
docs.text:
The engine classifies the submitted code's algorithm using a three-tier approach, falling through to the next tier when confidence is insufficient:

  1. CodeBERT Transformer -- A fine-tuned CodeBERT model analyses the code's semantic structure and classifies it against a taxonomy of known algorithm families (e.g., search, optimisation, factorisation, simulation). This tier provides the highest accuracy when confident.

  2. ML Ensemble Fallback -- When the transformer's confidence falls below a threshold, an ensemble of scikit-learn classifiers (trained on extracted code features) provides a secondary classification.

  3. Rule-Based Pattern Matching -- As a final fallback, the engine applies heuristic pattern-matching rules that look for characteristic code structures (e.g., nested loops for matrix multiplication, recursive divide-and-conquer patterns).

docs.title: Key Modules
docs.text:
The engine is organised into the following core modules:

  • language_detector -- Identifies the programming language and quantum framework.
  • ast_builder -- Parses source code into an Abstract Syntax Tree using tree-sitter.
  • complexity_analyzer -- Computes classical complexity metrics from the AST.
  • quantum_analyzer -- Extracts quantum-specific metrics from quantum circuits.
  • algorithm_detector -- Orchestrates the three-tier classification pipeline.
  • codebert_algorithm_classifier -- Wraps the fine-tuned CodeBERT model for algorithm classification.
  • canonical_ir_builder -- Constructs a canonical intermediate representation for cross-language analysis.

docs.title: Optimization Suggestions
docs.text: Alongside the analysis report, the engine generates actionable optimization recommendations. Each suggestion identifies a specific area of improvement (e.g., reducing circuit depth, minimising CX gate usage, restructuring nested loops) and includes an improvement estimate quantifying the expected benefit. These suggestions help users refine their code before execution.

docs.title: Decision Engine
docs.text: Directory /decision-engine Stack FastAPI, Python, scikit-learn, XGBoost Port 8003

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Decision Engine is responsible for determining the optimal execution target for a given piece of code. It consumes the structured analysis report produced by the Code Analysis Engine, builds a feature vector, and runs it through a trained ML classification model. The output is a routing decision: execute on a real quantum device, a quantum simulator, or a classical machine.

docs.title: Feature Vector
docs.text:
The engine constructs a 10-feature input vector from the analysis report. The features capture the key dimensions that influence routing:

  • Problem type (algorithm family classification)
  • Overall code complexity score
  • Qubit count required
  • Circuit depth
  • Single-qubit gate count
  • Multi-qubit gate count
  • CX gate ratio
  • Available memory estimate
  • Time complexity class
  • Entanglement score

Each feature is normalised before being passed to the model, ensuring consistent scaling regardless of the input code's size.

docs.title: Prediction
docs.text:
The Decision Engine uses trained classification models (scikit-learn pipelines and XGBoost) to predict the optimal execution target. The model outputs one of three classes:

  • Quantum Device -- The code is suitable for execution on real quantum hardware, with expected quantum advantage.
  • Quantum Simulator -- The code involves quantum operations but is better served by a simulator (e.g., too many qubits for available hardware, or noise would degrade results).
  • Classical -- The code does not benefit from quantum execution and should run on a classical machine.

The prediction includes a confidence score, allowing downstream services to handle low-confidence decisions appropriately.

docs.title: Model Metadata
docs.text: The engine exposes metadata about the active model for operator inspection and debugging. This includes the model version identifier, training date, feature configuration, and performance metrics from the most recent evaluation run. Operators can use this information to verify that the expected model is deployed and performing within acceptable thresholds.

docs.title: API
docs.text:
The Decision Engine exposes the following endpoints:

  • /predict -- Accepts an analysis report and returns the routing decision with confidence score.
  • /health -- Returns the service health status, used by load balancers and monitoring systems.
  • /model-info -- Returns metadata about the currently loaded model, including version, configuration, and evaluation metrics.

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text: Directory /frontend Stack React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui Port 5173

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Frontend is a single-page application that provides the user-facing interface for the Nexar platform. It communicates exclusively with the API Gateway, presenting code analysis results, routing decisions, execution outputs, and hardware status in a cohesive experience. The application is built with React 18 and TypeScript, bundled with Vite, and styled using Tailwind CSS with shadcn/ui components.

docs.title: Code Submission and Analysis
docs.text: Users write or paste code into a syntax-highlighted editor and submit it for analysis. The editor supports multiple languages and quantum frameworks. Once analysis completes, the results are displayed inline, including language detection, complexity metrics, algorithm classification, and optimization suggestions.

docs.title: Decision Visualization
docs.text: After the Decision Engine routes the code, the frontend renders the routing decision alongside the metrics that influenced it. Users can see whether their code was directed to a quantum device, simulator, or classical machine, along with the confidence score and the feature values that drove the decision.

docs.title: Hardware Monitoring
docs.text: A dedicated view displays live device status and specifications for all quantum backends managed by the Hardware Abstraction Layer. Users can inspect qubit counts, connectivity maps, gate sets, and availability before submitting jobs to a specific device.

docs.title: Execution History
docs.text: Past jobs are listed with their results and performance data, including measurement distributions, execution times, and provider details. Users can revisit previous analyses and compare outcomes across different execution targets.

docs.title: AI Code Conversion Interface
docs.text: Users can invoke the AI Code Converter directly from the frontend to translate classical Python into quantum circuits. The interface displays the generated circuit alongside suitability scores and expected speedup estimates, and allows users to execute the converted circuit without leaving the page.

docs.title: AST Pattern Analyser
docs.text: An interactive view allows users to explore the Abstract Syntax Tree of their submitted code and see which patterns the platform identified as quantum-amenable. This transparency helps users understand why their code was routed to a particular backend.

docs.title: UI Libraries
docs.text:
The frontend relies on the following libraries for its component layer and data management:

  • Radix UI -- Accessible, unstyled primitive components used as the foundation for shadcn/ui.
  • TanStack Query -- Server state management for data fetching, caching, and synchronisation with backend services.
  • Recharts -- Charting library used for visualising measurement distributions, complexity metrics, and performance data.
  • Zod -- Schema validation for form inputs and API response parsing.
  • React Router -- Client-side routing for navigation between views.

docs.title: Hardware Abstraction Layer
docs.text: Directory /hardware-abstraction-layer Stack FastAPI, Python, Qiskit, Amazon Braket SDK, Azure Quantum SDK Port 8004

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) provides a provider-agnostic interface to quantum backends. It decouples the rest of the Nexar platform from the specifics of any single quantum cloud provider, allowing circuits to be submitted, monitored, and retrieved through a unified API regardless of whether the underlying hardware is IBM, AWS, or Azure.

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text:
The HAL integrates with three major quantum cloud providers:

  • IBM Qiskit -- Access to IBM Quantum real devices and simulators. Circuits are transpiled using the Qiskit transpiler to match the target device's gate set and connectivity.
  • AWS Braket -- Access to Amazon Braket managed quantum devices and on-demand simulators. The HAL uses the Amazon Braket SDK to submit tasks and retrieve results.
  • Azure Quantum -- Access to Microsoft Azure Quantum backends. The HAL uses the Azure Quantum SDK to interact with available quantum hardware and simulators.

docs.title: Device Management
docs.text:
The HAL maintains an up-to-date view of available quantum devices across all providers. For each device, it exposes:

  • Qubit count -- The number of qubits available on the device.
  • Status -- Whether the device is online, offline, or undergoing maintenance.
  • Connectivity -- The qubit coupling map, describing which qubits can interact directly.
  • Gate set -- The native gate set supported by the device.

This information allows the Decision Engine and other services to make informed choices about which device to target for a given circuit.

docs.title: Job Execution
docs.text:
The HAL accepts quantum circuits or classical code and submits them to the selected provider. Key execution parameters include:

  • Target device or simulator -- Specified by provider and device identifier.
  • Shot count -- The number of times the circuit is executed, configurable per job.
  • Transpilation options -- Optimisation level and layout method can be specified for providers that support transpilation.

The HAL translates the submission into the provider's native format, handles authentication with the provider's API, and returns a job identifier for tracking.

docs.title: Job Lifecycle
docs.text:
Each submitted job follows a managed lifecycle:

  • Submission -- The job is dispatched to the provider and a unique job ID is returned.
  • Status Tracking -- The HAL polls the provider for job status updates (queued, running, completed, failed) and exposes this through its API.
  • Result Retrieval -- Once a job completes, the HAL fetches the measurement results and normalises them into a standard format.
  • Performance Monitoring -- Execution metadata (queue time, execution time, shot count, provider-reported fidelity) is recorded for each job.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
The service is organised under the /app directory with the following layout:

  • api/routes/ -- FastAPI route definitions for device listing, job submission, status checks, and result retrieval.
  • providers/ -- Provider-specific adapters for IBM Qiskit, AWS Braket, and Azure Quantum, each implementing a common interface.
  • services/factory -- Factory pattern for instantiating the correct provider adapter based on the requested backend.
  • models/ -- Pydantic models for request/response schemas, device specifications, and job metadata.
  • messaging/ -- Internal messaging utilities for asynchronous job notifications.
  • core/ -- Shared configuration, constants, and utility functions.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get Nexar running locally in minutes. This guide provides copy-paste commands to launch all services.

docs.title: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Silverviles/nexar.git
cd nexar

docs.title: Start All Services
docs.text:
Open six separate terminals and run one command in each:

Terminal 1 -- API Gateway

cd api && npm install && npm run dev

Terminal 2 -- AI Code Converter

cd ai-code-converter && python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt && uvicorn main:app --port 8001 --reload

Terminal 3 -- Code Analysis Engine

cd code-analysis-engine && python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt && uvicorn main:app --port 8002 --reload

Terminal 4 -- Decision Engine

cd decision-engine && python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt && uvicorn main:app --port 8003 --reload

Terminal 5 -- Hardware Abstraction Layer

cd hardware-abstraction-layer && python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt && uvicorn main:app --port 8004 --reload

Terminal 6 -- Frontend

cd frontend && npm install && npm run dev

Windows users

Replace source .venv/bin/activate with .venv\Scripts\activate in the Python service commands.

docs.title: Verify
docs.text:
Once all services are running, confirm each endpoint responds:

Service Health Check URL Expected Response API Gateway http://localhost:3000/ JSON welcome message AI Code Converter http://localhost:8001/ {"message": "Hello World"} Code Analysis Engine http://localhost:8002/ {"message": "Hello World"} Decision Engine http://localhost:8003/ {"message": "Hello World"} Hardware Abstraction Layer http://localhost:8004/ {"message": "Hello World"} Frontend http://localhost:5173/ React application

Open http://localhost:5173/ in your browser to access the Nexar interface.

docs.title: Running Order
docs.text:
To run the full application, start services in separate terminals in the following order:

  1. API Gateway (Terminal 1)
  2. Python backend services (Terminals 2--5) -- start whichever services you need
  3. Frontend (Terminal 6)

The frontend depends on the API Gateway, which in turn proxies requests to the backend services. Start the API Gateway first so downstream services are available when the frontend loads.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Port already in use

  • For Python services, change the port with the --port flag: uvicorn main:app --port 8005 --reload
  • For the API Gateway, set the PORT environment variable: PORT=3001 npm run dev

Python virtual environment issues

  • Ensure the virtual environment is activated: source .venv/bin/activate
  • On Windows, use .venv\Scripts\activate instead

Missing dependencies

  • For Node.js services (api, frontend), run npm install
  • For Python services, run pip install -r requirements.txt

TypeScript errors

  • Confirm you are in the correct directory
  • Delete node_modules and run npm install again

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Nexar is deployed on Google Cloud Platform using a fully automated infrastructure-as-code approach. All services are containerized and managed through Terraform and GitHub Actions for continuous integration and delivery.

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text: Terraform manages all GCP resources in the /terraform directory. The infrastructure is defined declaratively, enabling reproducible deployments and version-controlled configuration changes.

docs.title: Services
docs.text: All 6 services are containerized with Docker and deployed to Cloud Run as independently scalable microservices.

docs.title: Key Resources
docs.text:
• Cloud Run: Hosts all microservices as independently scalable containers, providing automatic scaling and managed infrastructure.
  • Artifact Registry: Stores Docker images for all service containers, enabling versioned and secure image management.
  • IAM: Manages service account permissions, enforcing least-privilege access across all deployed resources.
  • Cloud Storage: Stores ML models and analysis artifacts used by the Decision Engine and Code Analysis Engine.

docs.title: CI/CD
docs.text: GitHub Actions workflows handle automated testing and deployment. On each push to the main branch, workflows build container images, push them to Artifact Registry, and deploy updated services to Cloud Run.

docs.title: Configuration Files
docs.text:
The Terraform configuration is organized into the following files within the /terraform directory:

  • cloudrun.tf — Cloud Run service definitions and scaling configuration
  • iam.tf — Service account and permission bindings
  • artifact_registry.tf — Container image repository configuration
  • variables.tf — Parameterized infrastructure variables

docs.title: NEXAR: Intelligent Quantum-Classical Workload Routing Platform
docs.text:
Prasad H G A T$^1$, Jayasinghe Y L$^1$, Hettiarachchi S R$^1$, Siriwardhana A H L T S$^1$

$^1$ Sri Lanka Institute of Information Technology (SLIIT)

docs.title: Abstract
docs.text:
Determining whether a computational workload benefits from quantum or classical execution remains a key challenge in the current Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) era. This paper presents Nexar, an intelligent workload routing platform that analyzes source code characteristics and predicts the optimal execution target by jointly optimizing for cost and execution time. The system employs a hybrid decision framework combining: (1) a machine learning ensemble trained on a 16-feature vector capturing circuit complexity, entanglement properties, and NISQ viability metrics; (2) a physics-aware rule-based engine enforcing hardware constraints and algorithm-specific routing logic; and (3) a real-time cost analyzer estimating execution time and monetary cost across multiple quantum providers and classical compute. These components are unified through weighted voting to produce hardware recommendations with confidence scores. The platform incorporates automated code analysis --- including algorithm classification via CodeBERT and quantum circuit characterization --- to extract workload features without manual annotation. We evaluate the system across 30 diverse workload types including search, factorization, optimization, and simulation problems, demonstrating that the framework consistently identifies cost-effective and time-efficient execution targets while serving as a practical decision-support tool for hybrid quantum-classical computing environments.

Keywords: quantum computing, workload routing, NISQ, decision engine, hybrid computing, cost analysis, CodeBERT, quantum advantage

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
The advent of Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices [1] has created an unprecedented challenge in computational resource allocation: given a workload, should it execute on classical hardware, a quantum simulator, or a real quantum device? This decision is non-trivial because it depends on a complex interplay of factors including problem structure, circuit complexity, hardware noise characteristics, execution cost, and available qubit counts.

Current quantum devices --- such as IBM's 127-qubit Eagle processors --- operate in a regime where noise, limited connectivity, and decoherence constrain the problems that can be solved with quantum advantage. Classical simulation of quantum circuits remains feasible for systems up to approximately 50 qubits, beyond which memory requirements grow exponentially as $O(2^n)$. This creates a crossover point where classical simulation becomes intractable and quantum execution becomes the only viable path.

However, simply having a quantum device does not guarantee advantage. The overhead of transpilation, measurement shots, queue times, and error rates means that for many problems --- particularly those with polynomial classical solutions --- classical execution remains orders of magnitude faster and cheaper. The challenge, therefore, is not whether quantum computing will eventually provide advantage, but rather when and for which problems it does so today.

This paper addresses this challenge by presenting Nexar, a full-stack platform that automates the analysis, classification, and routing of computational workloads across quantum and classical execution backends. Nexar's contributions are:

  1. Automated Code Analysis Pipeline: A multi-dimensional analysis engine that detects programming languages across five quantum and classical frameworks, computes complexity metrics, and classifies algorithms using a three-tier approach combining CodeBERT transformers, ensemble machine learning, and rule-based pattern matching.

  2. Hybrid Decision Engine: A three-component decision framework that merges ML predictions (50% weight), physics-aware rules (35% weight), and real-time cost analysis (15% weight) through weighted voting to produce hardware recommendations with calibrated confidence scores.

  3. Provider-Agnostic Execution Layer: A hardware abstraction layer supporting IBM Qiskit, AWS Braket, and Azure Quantum through a unified API, enabling transparent workload routing across heterogeneous quantum backends.

  4. Empirical Evaluation: A comprehensive benchmark across 30 workloads spanning classical computation, hybrid quantum-classical algorithms, and pure quantum circuits, with cost and performance analysis demonstrating the system's routing accuracy and the current state of quantum-classical cost tradeoffs.

docs.title: 2.1 Quantum-Classical Hybrid Frameworks
docs.text: Several frameworks have emerged to bridge quantum and classical computing. Qiskit Runtime [2] provides session-based quantum execution with classical co-processing. Amazon Braket [3] offers a multi-provider interface but lacks automated workload analysis. Azure Quantum [4] provides resource estimation but does not perform code-level analysis for routing decisions. Nexar differentiates itself by integrating code analysis, decision-making, and execution into a single automated pipeline.

docs.title: 2.2 Quantum Resource Estimation
docs.text: Resource estimation for quantum algorithms has been studied extensively. Beverland et al. [5] developed methods for estimating logical qubit requirements for fault-tolerant algorithms. However, these approaches focus on future error-corrected devices rather than current NISQ hardware. Nexar's cost analyzer operates on real NISQ pricing models and hardware constraints, providing immediately actionable cost estimates.

docs.title: 2.3 Algorithm Classification and Code Analysis
docs.text: Static code analysis for quantum programs has been explored in works such as ScaffCC [6] and Q# compiler toolchains. CodeBERT [7], a pre-trained model for programming language understanding, has shown strong performance on code classification tasks. Nexar extends these approaches by combining AST-based analysis with CodeBERT for quantum algorithm detection, enabling classification across five quantum programming frameworks.

docs.title: 2.4 Quantum Advantage Thresholds
docs.text: The question of when quantum advantage emerges has been studied through computational complexity theory [8] and empirical benchmarks [9]. Our work contributes empirical data on cost-based crossover points using real provider pricing, demonstrating that the quantum advantage threshold is approximately 50--60 qubits for cost parity and beyond 120 qubits for clear quantum superiority in simulation tasks.

docs.title: 3. System Architecture
docs.text: Nexar is implemented as a microservices monorepo comprising six independently deployable services communicating through a central API Gateway. The architecture is designed for modularity, allowing each component to scale independently and be updated without affecting the overall system.

docs.title: 3.1 Architecture Overview
docs.text:
The system follows a pipeline architecture with the following request flow:

  1. Code Submission: Users submit source code through a React-based frontend.
  2. Authentication and Routing: An Express.js API Gateway authenticates requests via JWT and routes them to backend services.
  3. Code Analysis: The Code Analysis Engine performs multi-dimensional analysis including language detection, complexity computation, and algorithm classification.
  4. Routing Decision: The Decision Engine consumes analysis metrics and produces a hardware recommendation.
  5. Code Conversion (optional): If quantum execution is selected and the code is classical, the AI Code Converter translates it to quantum circuits.
  6. Execution: The Hardware Abstraction Layer submits jobs to the selected quantum or classical backend.

docs.title: 3.2 Service Specifications
docs.text:
Service Stack Port Function Frontend React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS 5173 User interface, visualization API Gateway Express 5, TypeScript 3000 Authentication, proxying, logging Code Analysis Engine FastAPI, Python, CodeBERT, tree-sitter 8002 Language detection, metrics, classification Decision Engine FastAPI, Python, scikit-learn, XGBoost 8003 ML-based routing prediction AI Code Converter FastAPI, Python, Transformers 8001 Classical-to-quantum translation Hardware Abstraction Layer FastAPI, Python, Qiskit, Braket SDK 8004 Multi-provider job execution

All services are containerized with Docker and deployed to Google Cloud Platform via Cloud Run, with infrastructure managed through Terraform.

docs.title: 4. Code Analysis Engine
docs.text: The Code Analysis Engine serves as the analytical foundation of the pipeline, transforming raw source code into a structured feature set that downstream components consume.

docs.title: 4.1 Language Detection
docs.text: The engine supports five programming languages and frameworks: Python, Qiskit, Cirq, Q#, and OpenQASM. Detection uses an ML-based classifier achieving confidence scores above 0.85 across all evaluated workloads. In our benchmark, average language detection confidence was 0.955 (sd = 0.028) across 30 workloads.

docs.title: 4.2 Classical Metrics Extraction
docs.text:
For all detected code, the engine computes classical software metrics using tree-sitter-based AST parsing:

  • Cyclomatic Complexity: Measures the number of independent execution paths. Values in our benchmark ranged from 2 to 31.
  • Cognitive Complexity: Assesses human comprehension difficulty, ranging from 0 to 39 across workloads.
  • Time Complexity Classification: Inferred from loop structure and recursion patterns --- classes include $O(1)$, $O(n)$, $O(n \log n)$, $O(n^2)$, $O(n^3)$, $O(n^k)$, and $O(2^n)$.
  • Space Complexity: Estimated from data structure allocation patterns.
  • Structural Metrics: Loop count, conditional count, function count, and nesting depth.

docs.title: 4.3 Quantum Metrics Extraction
docs.text:
For quantum code, additional metrics are extracted:

  • Qubit Requirements: The number of qubits needed for circuit execution.
  • Circuit Depth: The number of sequential gate layers, directly impacting decoherence.
  • Gate Count and CX Gate Ratio: Total gates and the proportion of two-qubit entangling gates (CNOT/CX), which are the primary source of noise on NISQ devices.
  • Superposition Score (0--1): Quantifies the degree of superposition in the circuit.
  • Entanglement Score (0--1): Measures entanglement density, a key indicator of quantum advantage potential.

docs.title: 4.4 Algorithm Classification
docs.text:
Algorithm detection employs a three-tier approach:

  1. CodeBERT Transformer: A fine-tuned CodeBERT model classifies the algorithm family (e.g., search, optimization, factorization, simulation) based on code semantics.
  2. ML Ensemble Classifier: A scikit-learn ensemble provides a secondary classification using extracted feature vectors.
  3. Rule-Based Pattern Matching: Deterministic rules identify known quantum patterns (e.g., QFT patterns, Grover oracle structures, VQE ansatz patterns).

The system selects the highest-confidence result, falling back through tiers when confidence is below threshold.

docs.title: 5. Decision Engine
docs.text: The Decision Engine is the central intelligence of Nexar. It implements a hybrid prediction system combining three components through weighted voting.

docs.title: 5.1 Feature Vector Design
docs.text:
The engine operates on a 16-feature input vector composed of 10 raw features and 6 derived physics features:

Raw Features (10):

# Feature Description 1 problem_size Number of elements or variables 2 qubits_required Quantum bits needed 3 circuit_depth Sequential quantum gate layers 4 gate_count Total quantum gates 5 cx_gate_ratio Proportion of entangling gates (0--1) 6 superposition_score Superposition potential (0--1) 7 entanglement_score Entanglement density (0--1) 8 memory_requirement_mb Classical RAM required 9 problem_type_encoded Categorical: factorization, search, optimization, etc. 10 time_complexity_encoded Complexity class: exponential, polynomial, etc.

Derived Physics Features (6):

# Feature Formula Purpose 11 Circuit Volume $qubits \times depth$ Quantum resource intensity 12 Noise Sensitivity $qubits \times depth \times cx_ratio$ Error accumulation prediction 13 Quantum Overhead Ratio $problem_size / qubits$ Encoding efficiency 14 NISQ Viability Score Composite penalty function Feasibility on current hardware 15 Gate Density $gate_count / qubits$ Operational complexity per qubit 16 Entanglement Factor $cx_ratio \times entanglement$ Combined entanglement potential

The NISQ Viability Score applies penalties when qubits exceed 50, circuit depth exceeds 1,000, or circuit volume exceeds 50,000, reflecting the practical limits of current hardware.

docs.title: 5.2 ML Model Component (50% Weight)
docs.text:
The ML component uses a Random Forest / Neural Network ensemble trained on 10,000+ synthetic problem instances. The model performs binary classification (Quantum=1, Classical=0) and outputs calibrated probability estimates.

Training Process: 1. Feature vectors are standardized using z-score normalization. 2. The model is trained with stratified cross-validation. 3. Output probabilities are calibrated using Platt scaling.

The ML component achieves approximately 87% accuracy on held-out test data with 50--100ms inference latency.

docs.title: 5.3 Rule-Based System (35% Weight)
docs.text:
The rule-based component encodes physics constraints and domain knowledge:

Hardware Capability Constraints: - Maximum qubits: 127 (IBM Eagle processor limit) - Maximum circuit depth: 10,000 - Minimum qubits for quantum routing: 2

Problem-Type Routing Rules: - Quantum-favorable: Factorization (Shor's, exponential speedup), Search (Grover's, quadratic speedup), Simulation (natural quantum simulation), Optimization (QAOA/VQE) - Classical-favorable: Sorting ($O(n \log n)$ classical optimality), Dynamic Programming (sequential dependencies), Matrix Operations (mature classical libraries)

Safety Constraints: - Maximum circuit volume: 1,000,000 - Maximum noise sensitivity: 50,000 - Minimum NISQ viability: 0.1

The rule system can issue four decision types: FORCE_QUANTUM, FORCE_CLASSICAL, ALLOW_BOTH, or REJECT. Force decisions override the ML component, ensuring that physically impossible or clearly suboptimal configurations are not recommended.

docs.title: 5.4 Cost Analysis Component (15% Weight)
docs.text:
The cost analyzer estimates execution time and monetary cost for both quantum and classical execution paths.

Quantum Cost Model (based on real provider pricing):

$$C_q = C_{base} + (t_q \cdot r_{QPU}) + (n_{shots} \cdot r_{shot}) + (n_{gates} \cdot r_{gate})$$

Where $C_{base} = \$0.30$ (job submission), $r_{QPU} = \$1.60/s$, $r_{shot} = \$0.00145$, $r_{gate} = \$0.0001$, and $n_{shots} = 1024$ (default).

Classical Cost Model (AWS EC2 approximation):

$$C_c = (t_c \cdot r_{compute}) + (M_{GB} \cdot t_c \cdot r_{memory}) + C_{overhead}$$

Where $r_{compute} = \$0.0001/s$ (~$0.36/hr), $r_{memory} = \$0.00001/GB \cdot s$, and $C_{overhead} = \$0.001$.

docs.title: 5.5 Decision Merging
docs.text:
The three components are merged through a weighted voting algorithm:

$$S_{quantum} = w_{ML} \cdot P_{ML}^{q} + w_{rules} \cdot P_{rules}^{q} + w_{cost} \cdot P_{cost}^{q}$$

where $w_{ML} = 0.50$, $w_{rules} = 0.35$, $w_{cost} = 0.15$.

Rule overrides (FORCE_QUANTUM, FORCE_CLASSICAL) take highest priority and bypass the weighted calculation. The final recommendation includes the hardware target, confidence score, rationale string, cost estimates, and alternative options with their trade-offs.

docs.title: 6. AI Code Converter
docs.text: The AI Code Converter enables the platform to accept classical Python code and automatically translate it into quantum circuits when quantum execution is recommended.

docs.title: 6.1 Translation Pipeline
docs.text:
The converter uses a transformer-based code generation model to produce quantum circuit code targeting Qiskit or Cirq frameworks. The translation pipeline operates in three stages:

  1. Logic Extraction: Core computational logic is isolated from boilerplate, I/O, and classical scaffolding code.
  2. Pattern Recognition: The system identifies quantum-amenable patterns and matches them to known quantum algorithms:
  3. Linear search $\rightarrow$ Grover's algorithm (quadratic speedup)
  4. Combinatorial optimization $\rightarrow$ QAOA (potential exponential speedup)
  5. Integer factorization $\rightarrow$ Shor's algorithm (exponential speedup)
  6. Circuit Generation: The transformer model generates the target quantum circuit with appropriate gate sequences, measurements, and classical post-processing.

docs.title: 6.2 Suitability Scoring
docs.text: Each translation candidate receives a suitability score (HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW) with associated confidence and estimated speedup. This allows users to make informed decisions about whether the quantum translation provides meaningful advantage.

docs.title: 7. Hardware Abstraction Layer
docs.text: The Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) provides a provider-agnostic interface for quantum job submission and management.

docs.title: 7.1 Supported Providers
docs.text: Provider SDK Capabilities IBM Quantum Qiskit 127-qubit Eagle processors, simulators AWS Braket Amazon Braket SDK IonQ, Rigetti, OQC devices Azure Quantum Azure Quantum SDK IonQ, Quantinuum devices

docs.title: 7.2 Job Lifecycle Management
docs.text:
The HAL manages the complete job lifecycle:

  1. Device Discovery: Queries available devices across all providers, exposing qubit counts, connectivity maps, gate sets, and current status.
  2. Job Submission: Accepts quantum circuits with execution parameters (target device, shot count, transpilation options) and submits through the appropriate provider SDK.
  3. Status Tracking: Monitors job state transitions (queued, running, completed, failed) across providers.
  4. Result Retrieval: Returns measurement distributions, performance metrics, and execution metadata through a unified response format.

The factory-based architecture allows new quantum providers to be added by implementing a single provider interface, without modifications to the routing or analysis logic.

docs.title: 8.1 Benchmark Design
docs.text:
We evaluated Nexar across 30 workloads organized into three categories:

  • Classical workloads (10): Neural network backpropagation, protein folding energy computation, molecular dynamics, genome sequence alignment, PCA dimensionality reduction, epidemic SIR modeling, FFT signal processing, k-means clustering, heat equation PDE solving, and decision tree classification.
  • Hybrid workloads (5): Quantum SVM kernel estimation, quantum Boltzmann sampling, variational quantum eigensolver (excited states), quantum GAN generator, and drug molecule screening.
  • Quantum workloads (15): $H_2$ molecule VQE, LiH molecule UCCSD, Ising model simulation, Heisenberg spin chain, Shor's factoring, QAOA portfolio optimization, variational quantum classifier, quantum walk on graphs, VQE water molecule, quantum error correction, GHZ state teleportation, Deutsch-Jozsa (8-qubit), quantum phase estimation, HHL algorithm, and 120-qubit VQE molecular simulation.

All workloads were executed through the complete Nexar pipeline, and analysis, decision, and timing metrics were recorded.

docs.title: 8.2 Routing Accuracy
docs.text:
The Decision Engine produced correct routing recommendations across all workload categories:

Classical Workloads (10/10 correct): All 10 classical workloads were routed to classical hardware with confidence 1.0. The rule system correctly identified these as quantum-incompatible due to zero qubit requirements (rationale: "Too few qubits (0 < 2)"), demonstrating that the safety constraints prevent unnecessary quantum execution.

Hybrid Workloads (5/5 appropriate): The hybrid workloads exhibited split routing. Three workloads with sufficient quantum characteristics (Boltzmann sampling, VQE excited states, quantum GAN) were routed to quantum hardware with confidence 0.85. The QSVM kernel estimation (4 qubits) was routed to classical despite quantum characteristics, reflecting the cost-aware decision that small circuits run more efficiently classically. Drug molecule screening was correctly identified as classical-dominant.

Quantum Workloads (15/15 appropriate): Of 15 quantum workloads, 10 were routed to quantum hardware (confidence 0.85--1.0) and 5 were routed to classical. The classical routing for small circuits (3--4 qubits: GHZ teleportation, HHL, $H_2$ VQE, quantum walk) reflects the correct assessment that NISQ overhead makes small quantum circuits more expensive than classical simulation. The 120-qubit VQE was routed to quantum with confidence 1.0 --- the only workload where classical execution is physically intractable.

docs.title: 8.3 Cost Analysis Results
docs.text:
The cost analysis reveals the current economic landscape of quantum vs. classical computing:

Table 1: Representative Cost Comparison

Workload Qubits Hardware Est. Cost (USD) Est. Time (ms) Neural Network Backprop 0 Classical $0.001 11.37 Protein Folding Energy 0 Classical $0.001 12.54 FFT Signal Processing 0 Classical $0.001 1.42 Decision Tree (O(2^n)) 0 Classical $0.001 460.84 QSVM Kernel Estimation 4 Classical $0.001 1.00 LiH Molecule UCCSD 12 Quantum $2.162 234.37 Ising Model (16q) 16 Quantum $2.321 319.42 Shor's Factoring (15) 8 Quantum $2.159 232.36 QEC Surface Code 17 Quantum $2.146 223.83 120-qubit VQE 120 Quantum $3.489 986.33

The data demonstrates a clear cost disparity: classical execution costs approximately $0.001 USD per workload, while quantum execution ranges from $2.12 to $3.49 USD --- a factor of approximately 2,000x. This reflects the current state of NISQ economics where per-shot costs, QPU time charges, and base job fees dominate.

docs.title: 8.4 Cost Break-Even Analysis
docs.text:
Despite the current quantum cost premium, analysis of the cost scaling behavior reveals a fundamental crossover point driven by the exponential memory requirements of classical quantum simulation.

For an $n$-qubit quantum state, classical simulation requires $2^n$ complex amplitudes, consuming approximately $2^n \times 16$ bytes of memory. This creates the following scaling:

Qubits Classical Memory Classical Feasibility 20 16 MB Trivial 30 16 GB Desktop 40 16 TB Cluster 50 16 PB Supercomputer limit 60 16 EB Physically impossible 120 $\sim 2 \times 10^{31}$ MB ---

The crossover occurs at approximately 50--57 qubits where classical simulation costs begin exceeding quantum execution costs, and by 120 qubits, the classical cost is estimated at $10^{50}$ USD (effectively infinite), while quantum execution remains at $3.49 USD.

This analysis identifies three zones: 1. Classical Advantage Zone (< 50 qubits): Classical hardware is ~1,900x more cost-effective. 2. Memory Wall (50--60 qubits): Classical simulation costs grow exponentially; crossover occurs. 3. Quantum Advantage Zone (> 60 qubits): Quantum execution is the only physically viable option for exact simulation.

docs.title: 8.5 Pipeline Performance
docs.text:
Table 2: Pipeline Timing Metrics

Metric Classical Workloads Quantum Workloads Avg. Analysis Time 731 ms 1,355 ms Avg. Decision Time 12 ms 10 ms Avg. Total Pipeline Time 1,222 ms 1,770 ms Language Detection Confidence 0.942 0.966

Quantum workloads require approximately 1.85x longer analysis time due to additional quantum metrics extraction (circuit parsing, entanglement scoring). Decision latency remains consistently below 15ms across all workloads, well within the 500ms requirement specified in the system design. Total pipeline throughput averages 1.5 seconds end-to-end.

docs.title: 8.6 Observations on Current Quantum Limitations
docs.text:
Our benchmark data honestly reflects the limitations of current NISQ hardware:

  1. No workload is currently cheaper on quantum hardware. Even the smallest quantum circuits ($\sim$3 qubits) cost approximately $2 USD on real hardware versus $0.001 classically. This is due to per-shot costs, queue times, and base job submission fees.

  2. Quantum hardware is consistently slower for small problems. A single quantum gate takes approximately 100--500 nanoseconds, and 1,024 measurement shots are required for statistical results. Classical CPUs perform billions of operations per nanosecond with deterministic results.

  3. The value proposition is forward-looking. Quantum advantage will emerge when:

  4. Error-corrected quantum computers arrive (estimated 2028--2035)
  5. Problem sizes exceed 50+ qubits for exact simulation

  6. Algorithmic speedups (exponential for factorization, quadratic for search) outweigh hardware cost premiums

  7. The routing framework is ready. When quantum hardware crosses the advantage threshold, Nexar's pipeline will automatically route eligible workloads to quantum backends --- the decision infrastructure is in place.

docs.title: 9. Deployment and Infrastructure
docs.text:
Nexar is deployed on Google Cloud Platform using a fully automated infrastructure-as-code approach. All six microservices run as independently scalable containers on Cloud Run, with Docker images stored in Google Artifact Registry. Infrastructure is defined declaratively in Terraform, organized across configuration files for Cloud Run services (cloudrun.tf), IAM policies (iam.tf), container registries (artifact_registry.tf), and parameterized variables (variables.tf).

CI/CD is handled through GitHub Actions workflows that lint, test, build, and deploy each service independently. Pull requests trigger preview deployments, while merges to the main branch trigger production deployments. ML models and analysis artifacts are stored in Cloud Storage and loaded at service startup.

docs.title: 10.1 Decision Engine Effectiveness
docs.text:
The hybrid decision approach --- combining ML predictions with physics-aware rules and cost analysis --- proves effective across the workload spectrum. The rule system's override capability is critical: it prevents the ML model from recommending quantum execution for zero-qubit classical problems and enforces hardware limits. The 87% ML accuracy, while not perfect, is compensated by the rule system's deterministic constraints, resulting in appropriate routing for all 30 benchmark workloads.

The weighted voting scheme (50% ML, 35% rules, 15% cost) reflects the current state of quantum computing where physics constraints and hardware limitations are often more decisive than learned patterns. As quantum hardware matures and the decision boundary becomes more nuanced, the ML component's weight may increase.

docs.title: 10.2 The Quantum Cost Paradox
docs.text: Our results reveal what we term the "Quantum Cost Paradox" in the NISQ era: the decision engine correctly identifies workloads that would theoretically benefit from quantum execution, but the actual cost on current hardware is always higher than classical. This paradox resolves at scale --- for 120+ qubit problems, quantum is the only viable option regardless of cost. The platform's transparency in presenting both theoretical advantage and practical cost is a feature that aids informed decision-making.

docs.title: 10.3 Limitations
docs.text:
1. Synthetic Training Data: The ML model is trained on synthetic problem instances rather than real quantum execution traces. As real quantum benchmark data becomes available, retraining on empirical data should improve routing accuracy.
  2. Static Cost Models: Provider pricing is encoded as configuration parameters. Real-time pricing integration would improve cost accuracy.
  3. Limited Problem Types: The current system focuses on optimization, search, simulation, and factorization. Extending to quantum chemistry, quantum machine learning, and cryptographic workloads would broaden applicability.
  4. NISQ-Era Constraints: The system is designed for current NISQ hardware. Fault-tolerant quantum computing will require updated decision logic, cost models, and hardware constraints.

docs.title: 11. Conclusion
docs.text:
We presented Nexar, an intelligent quantum-classical workload routing platform that addresses the fundamental challenge of execution target selection in the NISQ era. Through the integration of automated code analysis, hybrid ML-and-rules-based decision-making, and provider-agnostic quantum execution, the platform provides a complete pipeline from source code submission to hardware recommendation and job execution.

Our evaluation across 30 diverse workloads demonstrates that the system achieves correct routing decisions across all workload categories, with pipeline latency averaging 1.5 seconds and decision inference under 15ms. The cost analysis identifies a clear quantum-classical crossover at approximately 50--57 qubits, beyond which classical simulation becomes economically and physically intractable.

The platform's honest assessment of current NISQ limitations --- where no workload is yet cheaper on quantum hardware for small circuits --- combined with its readiness to route workloads when quantum advantage materializes, positions Nexar as both a practical decision-support tool for today and an automated routing infrastructure for the fault-tolerant quantum era.

docs.title: 12. Future Work
docs.text:
1. Real Quantum Execution Benchmarks: Execute benchmark workloads on real IBM Quantum and AWS Braket devices to validate cost and timing estimates against actual hardware performance.
  2. Adaptive Model Retraining: Implement a feedback loop where execution results are used to continuously retrain the decision model, improving routing accuracy over time.
  3. Error Mitigation Integration: Incorporate quantum error mitigation techniques (zero-noise extrapolation, probabilistic error cancellation) into the cost model to reflect the true cost of obtaining reliable quantum results.
  4. Multi-Objective Optimization: Extend the decision framework to jointly optimize for cost, time, accuracy, and carbon footprint, supporting user-defined priority weights.
  5. Fault-Tolerant Readiness: Develop decision logic and cost models for fault-tolerant quantum computers, preparing the platform for the post-NISQ era.
